body:
## Review Summary

Correct fix. The `(0, false)` case (zero children, no value) was falling through to the wildcard `_ => NodeRemoveResult::Mutated` arm at line 258, which violates the `remove()` contract — `None` signals an empty subtrie, and `Mutated` means the node is still alive. This misaligned `BranchNode` with `LeafNode::remove` and `ExtensionNode::remove`, which both return `None` when their subtrie becomes empty. Without this fix, `Trie::remove` (trie.rs:171) would never reset the root to `NodeRef::default()`, persisting a structurally empty branch node as the trie root.

Note: this case requires removing the last remaining child from a valueless branch — unlikely in typical state tries but reachable and should be handled correctly.

comments.body: Good placement — the early return sits between the children collection (line 210-215) and the restructuring match (line 219). One minor thought: you could alternatively add a `(0, false) => return Ok((None, value))` arm to the `match` below — the behavior is identical but it would keep all the restructuring cases in one place. Not a big deal either way.
